coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot based luminescent solar concentrator coupled with polymer dispersed liquid crystal device for smart management of solar spectrum
coredata.dc:creator: Mateen, Fahad
Ali, Mumtaz
Oh, Heemuk
Hong, Sung-Kyu
coredata.dc:description: The ability of polymer dispersed liquid crystals (PDLC) smart window to electrically control the visible and near-infrared light transmissions aids to reduce the artificial lightning, heating and cooling loads in the built environment. This study presents a complementary coupling of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (N-CQDs) based waveguide with PDLC smart window device that selectively harvests the ultraviolet light (UV) and near UV light for electrical energy generation without competing for the visible and near-infrared light. The hybrid device can be switchable between transmissive and opaque light scattering state while simultaneously generating electrical energy. Spectral characteristics of the light transmitted through the device confirm that UV photons are completely harvested while most of the visible and near-IR photons are transmitted. The colorimetry analysis of waveguide-coupled PDLC devices is done to realize their compatibility with visual comfort of individuals residing in the building with such windows. We eventually demonstrate that strong scattering effect during the OFF state of waveguide-coupled PDLC device is responsible for its improved optical and power efficiencies (~ 4.52% and ~ 2. 49% respectively) as compared to that in the ON state of PDLC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waveguides
Quantum dots
PDLC
Smart window
Ultra-violet light

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed management of the solar spectrum through N-CQDs based waveguide-coupled PDLC smart window. Grey filled area is a solar irradiance at direct and circumsolar conditions.

Schematic representation of N-CQDs based waveguide-coupled PDLC smart window (50 mm × 25 mm × 4.2 mm). Near UV light is absorbed by the N-CQDs and re-emitted at longer wavelengths. The emitted light is propagated towards edges of the waveguide by total internal reflection (TIR). Visible and near-IR light simply passes through the upper waveguide and interact with PDLC, which in OFF state, allows the strong scattering of light in all directions. The fraction of light resulting from the scattering also tends to trap inside waveguide depending on the angle of propagation. The concentrated emission from the edges can be used to sensitize an optically matched solar cell, placed at the edges (b).

Characterization of prepared N-CQDs and PDLC. (a) TEM images of N-CQDs, (b) XRD diffraction pattern of N-CQDs, (c) Normalized absorption and emission spectra of N-CQDs in 0.04% aqueous solution, and (d) Emission spectra of N-CQDs in 0.04% aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths.
Photoluminescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in aqueous solution at various concentrations. Optical path length (2 mm) is equal to the thickness of waveguiding medium of our final device.
PDLC backside emissions of N-CQDs based waveguide coupled with no PDLC and with PDLC. The driving voltage in ON state was kept constant at 50 V.

(a) Measurement results of color coordinates in the CIE L*a*b* color space for the determination of the outer appearance of waveguide-coupled PDLC device and (b) Color possibilities of transmitted light through ON and OFF states of waveguide-coupled PDLC device plotted on a CIE 1931 xy chromaticity diagram. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Comparison of I-V curves of the single waveguide without PDLC and waveguide-coupled PDLC (OFF state) and (b) I-V curve of waveguide-coupled PDLC at different driving voltages.
CIE color analysis of waveguide-coupled PDLC device in ON and OFF states for computing the outer appearance and color of indoor environment due to light transmissions through it.

row.entry: CIE L*a*b* coordinates for determination of outer color of device under sunlight
CIE 1931 chromaticity cordinates of the light transmitted through our device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photovoltaic parameters of N-CQDs based waveguide-coupled PDLC device.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot based luminescent solar concentrator coupled with polymer dispersed liquid crystal device for smart management of solar spectrum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Dongguk University-Seoul, 3 Pil-dong, Jung-gu, Seoul 100-715, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Nano Engineering, Hanyang University Seoul, 04763, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic and Nano Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ability of polymer dispersed liquid crystals (PDLC) smart window to electrically control the visible and near-infrared light transmissions aids to reduce the artificial lightning, heating and cooling loads in the built environment. This study presents a complementary coupling of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (N-CQDs) based waveguide with PDLC smart window device that selectively harvests the ultraviolet light (UV) and near UV light for electrical energy generation without competing for the visible and near-infrared light. The hybrid device can be switchable between transmissive and opaque light scattering state while simultaneously generating electrical energy. Spectral characteristics of the light transmitted through the device confirm that UV photons are completely harvested while most of the visible and near-IR photons are transmitted. The colorimetry analysis of waveguide-coupled PDLC devices is done to realize their compatibility with visual comfort of individuals residing in the building with such windows. We eventually demonstrate that strong scattering effect during the OFF state of waveguide-coupled PDLC device is responsible for its improved optical and power efficiencies (~ 4.52% and ~ 2. 49% respectively) as compared to that in the ON state of PDLC.

ce:section.ce:para: Building envelope technologies are significantly important in making the built environment more sustainable. Over the past few years, significant research activities have been directed toward achieving a dynamic light control in transparent building envelopes. The efforts essentially include the development of “switchable smart windows” as they offer significant benefits such as the efficient control over solar irradiance to minimize the heating and cooling load as well as lower the use of artificial lighting, due to more precise use of sunlight (Malara, 2014; Baetens et al., 2010; Chen, 2011; Zhou, 2013; Sekhar and Toon, 1998). To date, technologies available for smart windows include polymer dispersed liquid crystal (PDLC) (Cupelli, 2009; Khaligh, 2015; Gardiner et al., 2009; Binet et al., 2001; Kim, 2015), electrochromic (EC) (Runnerstrom, 2014; Piccolo, 2010; Jelle, 2013; Deb, 2001; Patil, 2016; Wang, 2018); thermochromic (Cao, 2018; Xu, 2018; Li, 2017; Chang, 2017; Gao, 2012), photochromic (Cannavale, 2013; Meng, 2013; Mongstad, 2011), and suspended particle device (Vergaz, 2008; Ghosh et al., 2015; Ghosh et al., 2016). Among these technologies, PDLC devices provide a fast switching speed, high transparency and durability. A typical PDLC device consists of microdroplets of nematic liquid crystals arbitrarily dispersed within the polymer matrix and allow the switching between opaque and transparent state by applying external power (Park and Hong, 2009; Fahad, 2016). Nematic liquid crystals are optically birefringent with the ordinary refractive index (no) and extraordinary refractive index (ne) along the different axes. The polymer film is optically isotropic with refractive index np. Without external voltage (OFF state), the optical axes of liquid crystal droplets are randomly aligned, resulting in refractive index mismatching of liquid crystals and polymer, which cause strong scattering of incoming light. When enough voltage is applied, the liquid crystals with positive dielectric anisotropy align their long axes parallel to the external electric field. In this case, the incoming light experiences only no in the liquid crystal droplets. The PDLC device appears transparent owing to the matched no and np. Imparting energy generating feature to such smart windows is a key factor that could allow them to be employed in a built environment in a more practical way.

Luminophore based waveguides typically termed as “luminescent solar concentrator” (LSC) are low cost devices that can be made transparent in the visible light range and supply electrical power thereby lowering the power required by PDLC. The concept of these waveguide based PDLC smart windows has been discussed previously (Khandelwal, 2015; Kendhale et al., 2013; Schiphorst, 2014). These studies demonstrate the dye-doped PDLC device which allows the electrical switching between transparent and opaque states while at the same time, a part of the incident light is absorbed by the embedded organic dye and emitted at a longer wavelength, becoming trapped and guided to the edges of the window from where it is converted into electricity using conventional solar cells. Recently, we have presented a novel configuration of energy generating PDLC smart window (Mateen, 2018). In contrast to other energy generating PDLC smart windows in which dyes are doped in the same PDLC device, our device separates the waveguide and PDLC parts using a small air gap thus allowing the enhanced light harvesting through the edges. However, all of these studies focus on the visible or near-infrared (near-IR) light for energy generation that PDLC smart window aims to regulate dynamically. Selectively harvesting ultraviolet (UV) or near-UV light for energy generation, on the other hand, avoids disrupting the regulation of visible and near-IR light. In normal PDLC, a part of UV light is absorbed by the polymer depending upon its absorption properties and the remaining portion is wasted during scattering and transmission mode.

The design strategy adopted in this study provides an additional UV protection to indoor environments. Moreover, it can also find application in horticulture especially in UV protective cultivation in greenhouses. By applying UV harvesting smart window as greenhouse cover helps to combat many challenges regarding crop infestations (Rathee, 2018). Moreover, the controlled light transmission using PDLC can be beneficial for increasing plant yield (Sol, 2018).

Citric acid anhydrous powder with 99% purity and ethylenediamine was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. De-ionized water with high purity (∼4.3 µS/cm at 20 °C) was used throughout the experiment. To fabricate PDLC, NOA65 (photoreactive monomer) was used as a matrix material and is purchased from Norland Inc. USA. The nematic liquid crystal mixture E7 was prepared by mixing 4-pentyl-4 biphenylcarbonitrile (5CB), 4-heptyl-4-biphenylcarbonitrile (7CB), 4 octyloxybiphenylcarbonitrile (8OCB) and 4-cyano-4-n-pentyl-p-terphenyl (5CT). All of these constituents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

N-CQDs were synthesized by following the previous literature with slight modifications (Qu, 2014). Briefly, citric acid (2.86 g) and ethylenediamine (2.52 ml) were dissolved in 68 ml of water. The solution was mixed with a mechanical stirrer followed by the sonication until a clear solution was obtained. The prepared solution was then transferred to 250 ml teflon-lined stainless steel container and was baked for four hours at 160 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the N-CQDs were filtered through a 1000D dialysis tube to remove traces of insoluble impurities. Because of the highly functional surface, N-CQDs were readily dispersed in water to make a homogeneous colloidal solution by sonication for few minutes.

The N-CQDs based waveguide being the upper part of our device was built from a custom-made glass cuvette with the internal dimensions 50 mm × 25 mm × 2 mm. N-CQDs solution was inserted into the cuvette using a syringe. To make the PDLC, E7 liquid crystal mixture was chosen because of its high positive dielectric anisotropy (Δε = +14.3) (Wu et al., 1984). E7 was mixed with photoreactive monomer NOA65 in the ratio of 60:40 followed by the continuous stirring for 5 h at 80 °C. The mixture was then injected into the 20 μm gap of ITO coated glass sandwich cells and allowed to cure under UV light (2.8 mW/cm2, 365 nm) for 20 min. In the end, the device with the final dimension of 50 mm × 25 mm × 4.2 mm was acquired by pairing the N-CQDs based waveguide with PDLC, keeping an air gap of 200 μm between them.

For Size distribution of N-CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEOL JEM 2100F) was used. Wide angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku D/MAX-2500) having the Cu Kα radiation was used to study the graphitic planes of N-CQDs. X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) of multi-scale ESCA 2000 system with an energy step size of 0.05 eV was employed for elemental analysis and the detailed bonding structure of each element. Absorbance and fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs were measured using UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary Eclipse) respectively. The optical and overall power efficiencies of the prepared devices were obtained by attaching their one long edge with the commercially available polycrystalline silicon solar cell and illuminating their top surface with a solar simulator (Mc-Science) having Xenon arc lamp of 160 W, as a light source equipped with filters to approximate Air Mass (AM) 1.5 global spectrum. The light intensity was calibrated with the KIER-calibrated Si-solar cell, before performing the efficiency test. International Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity coordinate and spectral analysis of backside transmitted light was obtained by using a spectrometer (Avantes, ULS2048) connected to an integrated sphere through a 1 m long optical fiber cable. The devices were placed on the entry port outside the integrated sphere and were illuminated by a light source as above. Finally, the appearance of energy generating windows was evaluated on the base of CIE (L*a*b*) color space using a spectrophotometer (Konica Minolta M-2500d, Tokyo, Japan).

The N-CQDs were obtained according to the experimental procedure described in the previous section. To obtain the information about the size and shape of the N-CQDs, TEM technique was employed. A circular morphology can be observed in the TEM images of N-CQDs (Fig. 3 (a)). It can be seen that the N-CQDs have a size distribution in between 2 nm and 6 nm. A magnified TEM image further clarifies the circular morphology and average size of N-CQDs (5.538 nm). Fig. 3(b) illustrates the XRD pattern of N-CQDs powder and shows a broad peak centered at 2θ of 21–22° which refers to 002 graphitic planes, with a calculated interlayer spacing 0.399–0.42 nm. The increase in the interlayer spacing as compared to natural graphite (0.335 nm) is because of less effective π-π stacking interaction owing to the rich intrastriatal defects and the presence of excessive functional groups on the surface (Sahu, 2012; Hu, 2014). Moreover, the broadness of XRD peak is due to the amorphous nature and small size of synthesized quantum dots (Gao, 2015). To extend the characterization of functional groups specifically the nitrogen doping, XPS analysis is described in the supplementary information (Fig. S1).

N-CQDs based waveguide absorbs UV light and re-emit it at a longer wavelength which is than trapped inside the medium via TIR and moved towards its edges. Almost 100% of near UV light is absorbed by N-CQDs based waveguide as can be seen from the transmission spectrum presented in supplementary information (Fig. S2). The unabsorbed light (visible and IR) simply passes through the waveguide and is regulated by the attached PDLC. Spectroscopic measurements were performed to determine the light harvesting range of N-CQDs. Fig. 3(c) shows the results of normalized absorption and emission spectra of N-CQDs in 0.04% aqueous solution obtained by UV–Visible and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. N-CQDs show absorption maxima at about 350 nm with a broad absorption spectrum that approximately spans from 280 to 400 nm with the absorption tail that extend up to 600 nm. This sharp absorption band of N-CQDs is attributed to π–π* transition of the CC bond while the extended tail is attributed to the n–π* transition that appears due the presence of amide and other functional groups on their surface (Rajender and Giri, 2016; Biroju et al., 2015). On the other hand, the emission spectrum ranges from 390 to 600 nm shows maximum peak at 450 nm. The Stokes shift, which is the difference between absorption and emission peaks, is one of the significant factors in determining the performance of light harvesting waveguides. Small stokes shift resulted from the overlapping of absorption and emission spectra causes the reabsorption losses (Otmar et al., 2014). The N-CQDs employed in our system specifically reduce the reabsorption losses due to the large stork shift (100 nm). Fig. 3(d) depicts the emission spectra of N-CQDs in aqueous solution with different excitation wavelengths. The maximum emission intensity is appeared at 454 nm using 350 nm excitation wavelength. However, the intensity decreased with the further increase in excitation wavelength which clearly indicates that the N-CQDs are more responsive in the UV range. Another feature of Fig. 3(d) is the red shifting of emission peaks with increasing excitation wavelengths. Thus, the spectroscopic measurements clearly illustrate that N-CQDs absorb UV light and re-emit at higher wavelength i.e. in blue light range that moves towards the edges of waveguide for utilization by coupled solar cells.

Concentration of N-CQDs in such waveguides is an important feature that needs to be optimized. For the purpose, different concentrations of N-CQDs in aqueous solution ranging from 0.01% to 0.05% were tested and their emission as a function of concentration is plotted in Fig. 4 . In all the cases, the optical path length was maintained to 2 mm which is equal to the thickness of waveguiding medium of our final device. It is observed that the emission intensity of N-CQDs increased with a linear trend till 0.04%. Further increase in the concentration of N-CQDs causes the lowering of emission intensity as shown in Fig. 4. The decreased emission intensity at higher concentration of N-CQDs can be explained by the collisional quenching phenomena (Song, 2014). Particularly, when the N-CQDs with the concentration of 0.05% were employed, collisional quenching occurred and their emission intensity decreased. Moreover, high optical density or turbidity at higher concentrations can also result in decreased emissions. Further, it is important to consider that the re-absorption losses in this system are minimized due to the large stoke shift of the N-CQDs as shown in Fig. 3(c).

To determine the spectral characteristics of the transmitted light, waveguide-coupled PDLC device was illuminated by white light equal to AM 1.5G spectrum and backside emissions in ON and OFF state of PDLC was measured using integrated sphere techniques as discussed in above section. No further reflector or white diffuser was placed at the backside in order to preserve the actual optical transmittance and to reproduce the situation of the energy generating PDLC smart window exposed to sunlight. Spectra of light transmitted through N-CQDs based waveguide coupled with PDLC and without PDLC window is displayed in Fig. 5 . UV light is barely seen by human eyes but covers the significant portion of total solar flux as illustrated in the grey dotted spectrum. It can be clearly seen that UV light is undetected in the backside transmission spectra of all the samples. Using the experimental configuration schematized in Fig. 2, these results suggest that the UV light is strongly absorbed by the N-CQDs, re-emitted and is waveguided towards the edges. On contrary, most of the visible and near-IR light is easily transmitted through the samples. Nevertheless, the photon flux of visible and near-IR transmitted light during the OFF state of PDLC is almost two times less than the ON mode and obviously in the absence of PDLC. Visible and near-IR light is not in the absorption range of N-CQDs but is scattered by the coupled PDLC in random directions. and has new angular-dependent probabilities of being directed towards its edges. Moreover, a fraction of this scattered light upon interacting the glass-air interface of a PDLC is also totally internally reflected provided that its angle of propagation is larger than the critical angle with the normal to the interface (Montgomery, 1988). The critical angle (θc) according to the Snell’s Law is given as θc = sin−1(nair/nglass). Besides this, some of the photons re-emitted by N-CQDs also fail to be trapped inside the waveguide by TIR. It also happens for the photons intersecting the waveguide surface at angles θi ≤ θc, where θi is the incident angle of the photons. These transmitted UV photons also interact with PDLC (OFF state) and gets a chance to be guided toward the edges of the device. Considering the design constraints of our device including device dimensions, it can be suggested that more light is totally internally reflected of the device during OFF state of PDLC.

In addition to imparting the aesthetic constraints to the architectural applicability of waveguide-coupled PDLC devices as building windows, color plays a critical role because of its potential to alter the visual comfort of the individuals living in the buildings with such windows. Partial absorption of the incident solar radiations modifies the chromatic coordinates of natural sunlight resulting in altered colors of the indoor environment. To quantify the color of indoor environment (related to transmissive color) as well as the outer appearance (related to reflective color) of waveguide-coupled PDLC device, we carried out a CIE (the International Commission on Color) color analysis and the results are displayed in Fig. 6 . The outer color (reflective color) of our device was evaluated using the CIE L*a*b* color space, which is the conventional approach for assessing the appearance of materials under standard illumination conditions (Hunter, 1948; Meinardi, 2015). The CIE (L*a*b*) coordinates of the prepared device have been shown in Fig. 6(a) and their corresponding values are listed in Table 1 . Here, L* defines the lightness of the color. A positive value of a* defines the redness of the color, while a negative value of a* describes the greenness. On the other hand, yellowness or blueness is expressed by coordinate b*, which is positive for yellow and negative for blue. As evident from the Fig. 6(a), the values of L* for waveguide-coupled PDLC are closer during ON and OFF state (48.75 and 52.31, respectively). However, a waveguide with no PDLC attached on backside shows the value of L* = 37.37 which is lesser than that coupled with PDLC. Fig. 6 further indicates that in all the cases, a* and b* coordinates are not much away from the white color. However, to be more precise, a waveguide-coupled PDLC device gives a slightly greenish yellow tone as compared to the waveguide without attached PDLC which shows greenish blue tone.

Furthermore, CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of light transmitted through all the samples were measured as shown in Fig. 6(b). It is clear that the CIE chromaticity coordinates of transmitted light are very close to the central point whose CIE 1931 coordinates are; x = 0.3111 and y = 0.333. Corresponding values of x and y coordinates of our samples are listed in Table 1. These results can be explained by the full transmission of visible and near IR light through our samples. It is generally accepted that the coordinate values on or close this central white points are considered as white color (Mateen, 2018). Colorimetry analysis of our waveguide-coupled PDLC device confirms that N-CQDs harvest only near UV light and the overall device does not allow a significant distortion to the solar spectrum i.e. it behave as uncolored. Thus, the strategy adopted in this study is fully compatible with visual comfort of individuals residing in the building with such windows.

To evaluate the energy generation feature of waveguide-coupled PDLC, we first focused on the calculation of the optical efficiency (ηopt), which is defined as the number of photons emitted from the edges of the waveguide divided by the number of photons incident on its top surface. ηopt is the key metric to evaluate the performance of waveguide based solar concentrator (Zhao, 2016). A general method to estimate ηopt is to attach a photovoltaic cell at the end of the waveguide and measuring its short circuit current as given in Eq. (1) (Debije and Verbunt, 2012; Banal et al., 2014; Flores Daorta, 2014; Hernandez-Noyola, 2012). In our case, we attached polycrystalline silicon solar cell on one long edge of the waveguide-coupled PDLC device.
(1)
η
opt
=
I
waveguide
c o u p l e d
P D L C
×
A
edge
I
solar
c e l l
×
A
top
where Iwaveguide coupled PDLC is the short-circuit current measured from the solar cell attached with the waveguide-coupled PDLC device and Isolar cell is the short circuit current measured from the same solar cell under direct illumination of the same light source. While Atop and Aedge are its top surface area and the area of its lateral face where the solar cell is attached. Ratio Atop / Aedge is generally termed as geometric gain. In case of our waveguide coupled PDLC, the geometric gain is 5.5. Additionally, we also calculated the overall power efficiency (ηoverall) of waveguide-coupled PDLC device using the formula given in Eq. (2) (Zhou et al., 2015).
(2)
η
overall
=
J
sc
×
V
oc
× F F
(3)
FF =
V
MP
×
I
MP
V
OC
×
I
sc
where Jsc is the short circuit current density, Voc is the open circuit voltage, FF is the fill factor calculated using Eq. (3) in which VMP and IMP are the voltage and current at the maximum power and Isc is the short circuit current. To obtain the results, a waveguide-coupled PDLC device was illuminated perpendicularly to its surface by the AM1.5G solar simulator (100 mW cm−2). Firstly, the solar cell was covered with black commercial tape, which contained a window, such that the 50 mm × 4.2 mm face of the cuvette could be placed in contact with the solar cell, while the remaining part of solar cell was blocked from receiving light. Secondly, because of the presence of PDLC with additional electrical wiring, the transparent tape was also used to maintain the mechanical stability of device.

The current-voltage (I-V) curves of waveguide-coupled PDLC device in OFF state and ON state at different voltages are depicted in Fig. 7 and their photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table 2 . According to the results, the best performance was shown during the OFF state of PDLC for which the ηopt and ηoverall are 4.52% and 2.49%, respectively. We also compared these efficiencies with only waveguide i.e. without coupled PDLC and found that ηopt and ηoverall are 2.81% and 1.35%, respectively, which are significantly less than that coupled with PDLC (Fig. 7(a)). Moreover, the efficiencies were also obtained during the ON state of PDLC by varying the driving voltage from 10 V to 50 V (Fig. 7(b)). It is revealed that ηopt and ηoverall of the device during ON state at 10 V are 4.09% and 2.22%, respectively, and continues to decrease with the increasing driving voltage during ON state. The most reasonable explanation of these results can be found by the light scattering nature of PDLC. In a PDLC, LC molecules are confined within the droplets and each droplet has an axis of symmetry (optic axis) determined by the orientations of these LC molecules within the droplet. Light polarized parallel to the optic axis scatters differently from light polarized perpendicular to the same axis and the overall scattering depends on LC orientation relative to the propagation direction of the incident light (Drzaic, 1993). In OFF state of PDLC, the orientation of the optic axis varies randomly from droplet to droplet, thus allows the light scattering in forward and backward direction. Light scattering phenomena of our waveguide-coupled PDLC device proves to be beneficial for increase in ηopt and ηoverall. Firstly, a small portion of backward scattered light from PDLC tends to be trapped inside the waveguide and moves through waveguide towards to its edges and is absorbed by the attached solar cell. Secondly, a forward scattered light upon interacting the glass-air interface of a PDLC also tends to be guided through TIR towards PDLC edges. On the other hand, during ON state of PDLC, the LC molecules are aligned to the direction of electric field. With the increasing voltage from 10 V to 50 V, tendency of LC molecules to align in the direction of electric field also increase. As a result, light transmission phenomena though PDLC dominates over scattering phenomena.

At present the power efficiency of waveguide-coupled PDLC device is low, however, our group is further working on enhancing the power efficiency by developing novel quantum dots and improved designs. The current objective of this study is to present the novel coupling scheme of waveguide-coupled PDLC window using UV light for the smart management of solar spectrum and in particular, to first demonstrate the use of quantum dots for imparting the energy generating feature to PDLC windows.

In this study, we presented the energy generating smart window device constituted by pairing N-CQDs based waveguide as UV harvester and PDLC as transmission modulator of visible and near-IR light. In particular, N-CQDs absorb the near UV light and re-emitted blue light (390–590 nm) which is then moved to the edges via TIR. Visible and near-IR light that is not in the absorption region of N-CQDs simply passed through the waveguide and interacted with PDLC. Experimental results suggest that during the ON state of PDLC, light is significantly transmitted through it due to the reduced scattering. However, in OFF state, it strongly scatters the light in all directions. It redirects some of the untrapped light back into the waveguide as well as scatters the fraction of visible and near-IR photons at random directions, giving them new angular-dependent chances of being directed towards the edges of the devices through TIRs. I-V measurements confirms that ηopt (4.52% ± 0.02) and ηoverall (2.49% ± 0.01) of N-CQDs based waveguide-coupled PDLC device are maximum during the OFF state of PDLC. During the ON state of PDLC, least ηopt (3.07%) and ηoverall (1.52%) are observed at 50 V which is almost near to the simple waveguide without PDLC. Moreover, the visual properties of N-CQDs based waveguide-coupled PDLC devices support the compatibility of using such devices as the building windows.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (20174030201520) as well as by the Technology development Program (C0531529) funded by the Ministry of SMEs and Startups (MSS, Korea).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2018.12.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
